sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Oppel to Schenker, dated April 27, 1933
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Oppel discusses the consecutive fifths in Beethoven’s Quartet Op. 74. -- He has been reading books on music by Güldenstein (which discusses Schenker) and Achtélik, neither of which he can recommend. -- A pupil of his has compiled a list of Schenkerian terms and hopes to present them in a radio broadcast.

tei:closer.tei:salute: Alles Gute u. die herzlichsten Grüsse

tei:p.tei:note: The text of Der Zukunft, year 5 (May 8, 1897), 261-65, in Oppel's hand

tei:div.tei:p: I am sending you here the passage that you seek. I can easily get hold of volumes of Die Zukunft in the university library at any time. Do you still remember Rosegger’s amusing encounter with Brahms, which R. published in the same year with the title “A Strange Gentleman”? Characteristic of Brahms’s modesty. Just write to me, saying what citations of the literature you still need for Free Composition. I shall, of course, fulfill all your requests immediately.

Many thanks for your letter of February 6! Yes, for Schweitzer there is no help. The literature on Bach has grown so much, and continues to grow such that no individual can possibly cope with it all. Even before that, Kretzschmar gave up preparing the second edition of Spitta’s biography. Moreover, none of these gentlemen is musically up to the task. – For the communication from Zuckerkandl, Bulthaupt and the Spaniard, heartfelt thanks.
How far has Jonas gotten with his book? Universal Edition is probably just as dishonest as it was in the past. What is the point of a volume without music examples?
Will Weisse stay in America? Things seem to look even worse there than they are here!

This morning I went to the Wagner festival. Muck conducted the preludes to Parsifal and The Mastersingers. Considering his age, still surprisingly good. Strangely, he looks like Richard Wagner: might this merely be a type of spiritual assimilation? Mayor Goerdeler and Max Schillings spoke there, too, the latter very indistinctly. The whole lacked the proper inner solemnity despite the external trappings; even the Chancellor was there, with a gigantic entourage of police protection, which reminded me of the period of Wilhelm II and had a downright czarist effect. The Prelude to Parsifal is, however, an impossibility in the concert hall. –

I have been studying Scriabin with great interest; he knew the music of Chopin thoroughly. Alas, he increasingly abandons the use of a plan in his later works and wallows in colors, becoming ever short-breathed; the melodies lack suppleness and the architecture goes missing. A pity. That comes from the accursed obsession with wanting to outdo Chopin, thanks to the crazy idea of eternal “progress.” The consequence is negative: decay and dissolution. –

tei:closer.tei:salute: All good wishes, and the most cordial greetings
to the two of you from all of us!

tei:p.tei:note: The text of Der Zukunft, year 5 (May 8, 1897), 261-65, in Oppel's hand
